passages.explanation: Mark opens with no birth narrative, no genealogy — just a title: the beginning of the good news about Jesus the Messiah, the Son of God. He immediately quotes Isaiah 40 and Malachi 3: I will send my messenger ahead of you, who will prepare your way — a voice of one calling in the wilderness, prepare the way for the Lord. John the Baptist appears in the wilderness, preaching a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins. The whole Judean countryside and all the people of Jerusalem go out to him. He's clothed in camel's hair with a leather belt — the uniform of Elijah. He proclaims: after me comes the one more powerful than I, the straps of whose sandals I'm not worthy to stoop down and untie. I baptise you with water, but he will baptise you with the Holy Spirit. Mark's Gospel is fast and urgent from the first line. No slow build — the Messiah is here, the forerunner is preaching, and the action has started.

Jesus comes from Nazareth in Galilee and is baptised by John in the Jordan. Just as he comes up out of the water, he sees heaven being torn open and the Spirit descending on him like a dove. A voice comes from heaven: you are my Son, whom I love — with you I am well pleased. At once the Spirit sends him out into the wilderness, where he's tempted by Satan for forty days. He's with the wild animals, and angels attend him. Mark compresses the temptation into two verses — no dialogue, no details. What matters is that Jesus was tested and came through. The word 'torn' for heaven opening is violent — the same Greek word Mark will use when the temple curtain tears at Jesus' death. God is breaking through.

After John is put in prison, Jesus goes into Galilee, proclaiming the good news of God: the time has come — the kingdom of God has come near. Repent and believe the good news! Walking beside the Sea of Galilee, he sees Simon and his brother Andrew casting a net into the lake. He says: come, follow me, and I'll send you out to fish for people. At once they leave their nets and follow him. Going a little farther, he sees James son of Zebedee and his brother John in a boat, preparing their nets. Without delay he calls them, and they leave their father Zebedee in the boat with the hired men and follow him. Mark's Jesus doesn't negotiate or explain — he commands, and people obey. 'At once' and 'without delay' set the pace for the entire Gospel. The kingdom has arrived and there's no time to waste.

They go to Capernaum. On the Sabbath Jesus enters the synagogue and begins to teach. The people are amazed — he teaches as one who has authority, not as the teachers of the law. Just then a man with an impure spirit cries out: what do you want with us, Jesus of Nazareth? Have you come to destroy us? I know who you are — the Holy One of God! Jesus commands sharply: be quiet! Come out of him! The impure spirit shakes the man violently and comes out with a shriek. Everyone is amazed: what is this? A new teaching — and with authority! He even gives orders to impure spirits and they obey him. News about him spreads quickly through the whole region of Galilee. The demons recognise Jesus immediately — they know exactly who he is. The humans are still catching up. Authority is the key word: authority in teaching, authority over spirits. Mark establishes both in the first public scene.

As soon as they leave the synagogue, they go to the home of Simon and Andrew, with James and John. Simon's mother-in-law is in bed with a fever. They immediately tell Jesus about her. He goes to her, takes her hand, and helps her up. The fever leaves her and she begins to wait on them. That evening after sunset, the people bring to Jesus all the sick and demon-possessed. The whole town gathers at the door. Jesus heals many who have various diseases and drives out many demons. He won't let the demons speak because they know who he is. After sunset — because the Sabbath has ended and people can now carry their sick. The whole town at the door gives a sense of the overwhelming demand on Jesus from the very start. He silences the demons — what scholars call the 'messianic secret.' Jesus controls the narrative about his identity.

Very early in the morning, while it's still dark, Jesus gets up, leaves the house, and goes off to a solitary place to pray. Simon and his companions go looking for him. When they find him, they exclaim: everyone is looking for you! Jesus replies: let's go somewhere else — to the nearby villages — so I can preach there also. That is why I have come. So he travels throughout Galilee, preaching in their synagogues and driving out demons. The crowd wants more miracles in Capernaum. Jesus refuses to be pinned down — his mission is proclamation, not establishing a healing centre in one town. He prays before making the decision, and the prayer leads him away from popularity toward purpose.

A man with leprosy comes to Jesus and begs on his knees: if you are willing, you can make me clean. Jesus is indignant — or deeply moved (the Greek is debated). He reaches out and touches the man: I am willing. Be clean. Immediately the leprosy leaves him and he's cleansed. Jesus sends him away at once with a strong warning: don't tell anyone. Go, show yourself to the priest and offer the sacrifices Moses commanded, as a testimony to them. Instead, the man goes out and begins to talk freely, spreading the news. As a result, Jesus can no longer enter a town openly but stays outside in lonely places. Yet the people come to him from everywhere. The healed man's disobedience — telling everyone — actually hinders Jesus' mission. The messianic secret isn't arbitrary; Jesus needs to control the pace so the crowds don't force a premature confrontation. The man's excitement is understandable, but it has consequences.
